text: All plants are inevitably exposed to sodium ions (Na⁺) when facing salinity stress. Sodium ions are then distributed within the symplast at the cell level and at the organ level. Even though sodium ions contribute to decrease the cellular water potential, their accumulation is still dangerous and must be tightly regulated. While the differences between salt tolerant and salt sensitive species remain poorly explained and are not necessarily linked to the type of transporters used, the regulatory capacity on uptake and internal deposition has been shown to differ between species. Recent research suggests that salt tolerance can be regulated at a huge number of levels, ranging from calcium signaling and cyclic nucleotides and reactive oxygen species signaling, as well as hormone regulation at the transcriptional and post-transcriptional levels. The aim of this review is to give an exhaustive view of the intra and intercellular Na⁺ dynamics, especially focusing on the regulatory aspects governing salt tolerance in the early phases of salt shock.
